coredata.dc:description: This study successfully synthesized high performance blue emissive carbon dots (ZrN-CDs) using citric acid, zirconium chloride, and ethylenediamine as precursors through a hydrothermal method. The resulting ZrN-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission characteristics, along with good water solubility, salt resistance, and photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms allow for the selective attenuation of ZrN-CD fluorescence by tetracyclines (TCs). Building on this effect, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed by integrating europium ions (Eu3 +), featuring an inverse fluorescence signal response to enhance detection accuracy through self-calibration. The fluorescence emission behavior of ZrN-CDs (F440) showed selective quenching in response to TCs, while the characteristic emission of Eu3+ (F615) exhibited fluorescence enhancement due to the antenna effect (AE). Quantification of common TCs antibiotics, including tetracycline (TET) and oxytetracycline (OTC), was achieved by recording the fluorescence intensity ratio (F615/F440). The limits of detection (LOD) for TET and OTC reached 0.029 μM and 0.016 μM, respectively. This method was successfully applied to the detection of TET in bovine, goat, and camel milk, with recovery rates ranging from 83.65 % to 106.60 %, thereby confirming the reliability and effectiveness of the sensor in practical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study successfully synthesized high performance blue emissive carbon dots (ZrN-CDs) using citric acid, zirconium chloride, and ethylenediamine as precursors through a hydrothermal method. The resulting ZrN-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission characteristics, along with good water solubility, salt resistance, and photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms allow for the selective attenuation of ZrN-CD fluorescence by tetracyclines (TCs). Building on this effect, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed by integrating europium ions (Eu3 +), featuring an inverse fluorescence signal response to enhance detection accuracy through self-calibration. The fluorescence emission behavior of ZrN-CDs (F440) showed selective quenching in response to TCs, while the characteristic emission of Eu3+ (F615) exhibited fluorescence enhancement due to the antenna effect (AE). Quantification of common TCs antibiotics, including tetracycline (TET) and oxytetracycline (OTC), was achieved by recording the fluorescence intensity ratio (F615/F440). The limits of detection (LOD) for TET and OTC reached 0.029 μM and 0.016 μM, respectively. This method was successfully applied to the detection of TET in bovine, goat, and camel milk, with recovery rates ranging from 83.65 % to 106.60 %, thereby confirming the reliability and effectiveness of the sensor in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TCs), produced by Streptomyces species, display excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial properties against pathogenic microorganisms (Girmatsion et al., 2021). Tetracycline (TET) and oxytetracycline (OTC), known for their cost-effectiveness, easy availability, and broad-spectrum antibacterial properties, are widely used in animal healthcare for preventing and treating bacterial diseases and as feed additives in substantial quantities (Naik et al., 2017). The relative stability and persistence of OTC and TET contribute to their propensity to remain as residues in milk, thereby posing a great threat to human health (Ahmadi et al., 2015). Studies have shown that TET and OTC remain comparatively stable after processing at high temperature of 85 °C, with only a 5.7 % and 15.3 % reduction in activity, respectively (Kellnerová et al., 2014). Prolonged consumption of milk and dairy products with TC residues has the potential to induce liver damage, gastrointestinal disturbances, and impact the health of teeth and bones, emphasizing the importance of TC residue detection and regulation for dairy product safety (Hou et al., 2016). In this regard, the European Union (EU) has set the maximum permissible residue levels of TET and OTC in milk to 100 μg/kg, which is 0.225 μM for TET and 0.217 μM for OTC (Raza and Kim, 2018).

Traditional detection strategies for TCs include microbial assays, instrumental analytical techniques, and immunoanalytical methods (Fan et al., 2021; Gajda et al., 2017; Mahmoudi et al., 2014). Microbial methods are often limited by their lack of interference resistance and precision, making them primarily suitable for qualitative analysis. Instrumental analysis methods that combine various analytical techniques typically suffer from challenges such as high instrument and consumable costs, complex sample preparation steps, operational difficulty, and poor portability, making them less suitable for rapid on-site detection of TCs. Immunoassays are susceptible to the influence of antibodies and enzyme activity, which limits their practical application. In recent times, there has been a notable acceleration in the advancement of emergent detection technologies, which includes surface plasmon resonance sensors, surface-enhanced Raman scattering sensors, electrochemical sensors, and fluorescence detection techniques (Aminikhah et al., 2022; Babaei and Taheri, 2012; Feng et al., 2019; Khosravanian et al., 2021; Li et al., 2023b; Moslehipour, 2020; Ozkendir et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2020). Fluorescence sensors are acclaimed for their high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, simplicity, rapidity, and operability, positioning them as current focal points in detection research and ideal for tetracyclines (TCs) analysis (Yu et al., 2019). However, most modern fluorescent sensing platforms rely on single-emission signals, which can be affected by various factors such as environmental fluctuations, drift of lamps and detectors, local concentration variations of the probes, and autofluorescence in complicated biosystems. To address this challenge, ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on dual-emission signals collect fluorescence from two channels, with one channel serving as an internal reference. This approach incorporates built-in self-calibration capabilities, effectively reducing background interference and providing enhanced analytical precision (Zhang et al., 2021).

Carbon dots (CDs), owing to their facile synthesis, excellent water solubility, minimal toxicity, easy functionalization and surface modification, have been widely adopted as fluorescent probes in the development of fluorescence-based sensors (He et al., 2020). As of now, a variety of materials, including natural substances and synthetic compounds, have been employed as carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs through methods such as hydrothermal processes, microwave techniques, and solvothermal methods (Innocenzi and Stagi, 2023; Li et al., 2023a). Among these, the hydrothermal method has gained popularity due to its simplicity, uniform particle size, environmental friendliness, and high quantum yield (Xie et al., 2017). The fluorescence quantum yield is a critical determinant of the applicability and efficiency of CDs. Studies have demonstrated that CDs modified with heteroatoms may exhibit higher quantum yields and present spectral and localized chemical properties distinct from ordinary CDs containing only C, H, and O, thereby enhancing their fluorescence performance (Landa et al., 2022; Xin et al., 2022). Furthermore, the synergistic effect among heteroatoms doped within CDs can generate unique electronic structures, thereby opening new possibilities for the enhancement of the optoelectronic properties of CDs and the construction of sensing systems through multi-element co-doping (Wang et al., 2019). Consequently, many studies have explored doping CDs with elements such as N, S, P, Cu, and Mg, achieving doped CDs with superior optical and electronic characteristics (Fan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). However, research on the synthesis of CDs co-doped with zirconium (Zr) and nitrogen (N) for the detection of TCs remains unexplored.

It has been reported that Lanthanide (Ln)-based luminescent materials exhibit unique spectral characteristics, such as optical stability, long luminescence lifetime, large Stokes shift, and sharp line-like emission bands (Tan et al., 2023). In particular, europium ions (Eu3+) can form stable luminescent complexes with TCs containing β-diketonate configuration (Xu et al., 2020). Energy transfer from TCs to Eu3+ ions effectively sensitizes the characteristic emissions of Eu3+, a phenomenon recognized as the "antenna effect" (AE) (Yao et al., 2022). However, the chelation process between TCs and Eu3+ often encounters a quenching effect due to the high-frequency vibrational coupling of the O-H bond in water molecules, leading to diminished emission from the TCs-Eu3+ complex and reduced sensitivity in TCs detection (Wei et al., 2019). To enhance sensitivity, CDs can function as effective ancillary ligands to protect the fluorescence of Eu3+ from quenching by water molecules (Yao et al., 2022). Additionally, the inherent fluorescence of CDs offers a reference signal for the ratiometric detection of TCs.

In this study, Zr and N co-doped carbon dots (ZrN-CDs) with superior luminescence properties were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, exhibiting strong emission at 440 nm (λex=350 nm). Upon the addition of TCs (TET and OTC), the fluorescence intensity of ZrN-CDs was notably diminished due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, the fluorescence of Eu3+ and TCs was amplified via AE, with ZrN-CDs acted as ancillary ligands to preserve the fluorescence of Eu3+ from water-induced quenching, also contributed to reducing background interference. Based on these principles, a dual-emission ratio fluorescent sensor was developed by integrating bi-functional ZrN-CDs and Eu3+ for the detection of TET and OTC ( Scheme 1). The presence of TCs significantly enhanced the fluorescence emission of ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system at 615 nm, concurrently diminishing the emission at 440 nm, thereby altering the ratio of fluorescence signals. Analysis of real milk samples demonstrated the feasibility of detecting TCs in various milk using the constructed ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ ratio fluorescent sensor, thereby affirming its utility for TC detection within dairy product.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), tetracycline (TET), oxytetracycline (OTC), kanamycin (KAN), gentamicin (GEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), florfenicol (FLO), sulfamethazine (SMD), ampicillin (AMP), and neomycin (NEO) were all procured from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Serine (Ser), arginine (Arg), tyrosine (Tyr), cysteine (Cys), tryptophan (Trp), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), galactose (Gal), lactose (Lac), trichloroacetic acid, trifluoroacetic acid, sodium tungstate, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate, europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) were acquired from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tris-HCl buffer (1 M) was purchased from biosharp. NaCl, NaOH, KCl, CaCl2, FeCl2, ZnSO4, and AlCl3 were purchased from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Oxalic acid, hydrochloric acid, sulfuric acid, acetonitrile (HPLC grade), and methanol (HPLC grade) were obtained from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Bovine milk and Goat milk were obtained from Xinong Saanen Dairy Goat Breeding Farm (Yangling, China). Camel milk was obtained from farm located in Bainhot, Alashan League, Inner Mongolia. Unless specified otherwise, all other chemical reagents used in the experiments were of analytical reagent grade and could be used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water purified via a TKA system (Germany, 18.2 MΩ cm).

The morphology and particle size of ZrN-CDs were characterized using a TECNAI G2 F20 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM, FEI, USA). Infrared absorption spectra of ZrN-CDs were recorded with a Vertex 70 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The scanning range was 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1, and the number of scans was 16. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations of ZrN-CDs were performed using a K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), with Al Kα radiation at an energy of 1486.6 eV. The crystal structure of ZrN-CDs was analyzed by a D8 ADVANCE A25 X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, Germany), using Cu Kα radiation, with a 2θ data collection range of 10°-80° and a scan rate of 5°/min. The ultraviolet absorption peaks were determined by a UV-2550 ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), while the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured using an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The quantum yield was measured using an FLS1000 Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The content of TCs in actual samples was determined using an Agilent 1100 high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system (Agilent Technologies, USA). The Zeta potential were determined by a Zetasizer Nano analyzer ZEN 3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The synthesis of ZrN-CDs was conducted via a refined hydrothermal method, adapting the protocol from a previous study (Zhang and Fan, 2021). The process entailed dissolving 1 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of ZrCl4 in 20 mL of ultrapure water, subsequently adding 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then sealed within a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 150 °C for 2 h. Then, the mixture was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, whereupon it underwent centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was subsequently passed through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter membrane, and the filtrate was then dialyzed against distilled water for 24 h by using a 3000 Da dialysis membrane. The dialyzed sample was then frozen for 12 h and subsequently lyophilized under vacuum conditions, resulting in the production of a light brown, solid powder. In addition, a 2 mM Eu3+ solution was prepared for subsequent use.
Detailed information on the morphology, functional groups, crystal structure, and optical characterization of ZrN-CDs were shown in section S1 of the Supporting Information.
The optimization details of the detection conditions, including ZrN-CDs concentration, Eu3+ concentration, pH, and reaction time, were shown in Section S2 of the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of TET and OTC
ce:section.ce:para:
For the fluorescence assays of TET and OTC, 60 μL of Eu3+ solution (2 mM) was mixed with 180 μL of ZrN-CDs at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. This mixture was then spiked with various concentrations of TCs (TET and OTC) standard solutions and brought to a final volume of 2 mL using Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 8). After incubation at room temperature for 3 min, the fluorescence spectrum was scanned from 370 to 680 nm upon excitation at 350 nm. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 615 nm to that at 440 nm (F615/F440) was employed for quantitative analysis. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using Equation 2:
(1) LOD = 3σ/k
where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples (N = 10), and k is the slope of the calibration curve.
The same procedure was applied for specificity and anti-interference tests, as well as for the analysis of actual milk samples. All experiments were performed in three parallel experiments.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ ratio-based fluorescence sensor in a milk matrix, common antibiotics (KAN, GEN, CHL, FLO, SMD, AMP, NEO) and potential interfering substances (Ser, Arg, Tyr, Cys, Trp, AA, Glu, Gal, Lac, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+) were tested. The effect of these interferents on the detection system was investigated using the fluorescence intensity ratio F615/F440. The concentrations of interfering substances were set to five times that of TCs.

Bovine milk, goat milk, and camel milk were selected as sample to assess the applicability of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ fluorescence sensor for the detection of TET and OTC using a standard addition method. A volume of 5 mL of milk sample was mixed with 2.0 mL of 10.0 % trichloroacetic acid solution (w/v). The mixture was subjected to vortex mixing for 1 min, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, and subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to facilitate lipid and protein removal. The pH was then adjusted to neutrality using a NaOH solution, and the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate any residual precipitates. The filtrate was subsequently diluted tenfold with ultrapure water and spiked with a series of tetracycline (TET) standard solutions to prepare samples with varying TET concentrations for the following experiments. The ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ fluorescence sensor was utilized to detect TET in the samples, and the results were validated by comparing them with the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method.
All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Data analysis and visualization were conducted using Excel and Origin 2018.

The morphology and size of ZrN-CDs were investigated using transmission electron microscope (TEM), and as shown in Fig. 1A, the synthesized ZrN-CDs are well-dispersed and exhibit a uniform spherical shape. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) displays visible lattice stripes, indicating a crystallinity at 0.21 nm lattice spacing. The average particle size of the ZrN-CDs, determined by analyzing 100 individual particles using ImageJ software, was found to be 3.10 nm (Fig. 1C). In addition, the crystal structure of ZrN-CDs was characterized by XRD, with a characteristic peak at 25° in Fig. 1D corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane of carbon-based material, confirming the good crystallinity of the ZrN-CDs (Wang et al., 2022).

The main functional groups on the surface of ZrN-CDs were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1E, the broad absorption bands centered at 3421 and 3111 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups (Tabaraki and Sadeghinejad, 2018). The strong absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching (Han et al., 2012) and the peak at 1360 cm−1 to C-N bending vibrations (Meierhofer et al., 2020). Additionally, the band at 1056 cm−1 represents C-O-C stretching (Li et al., 2014), indicating a high concentration of oxygen-containing groups on the ZrN-CDs. This contributes to the enhanced hydrophilicity and stability of ZrN-CDs in aqueous systems, facilitating better dispersion in water solutions.

The elemental composition and surface chemical properties of ZrN-CDs were further established via XPS. The XPS full spectrum presented in Fig. 1F displays four major binding energy peaks at 183.72 eV, 285.92 eV, 401.08 eV, and 531.77 eV, attributed to Zr3d, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. These peaks substantiate the presence of Zr, C, N, and O elements in ZrN-CDs, with their respective elemental contents determined to be 2.54 %, 54.61 %, 7.93 %, and 34.92 %. Specifically, Fig. 1G illustrates the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which exhibits three distinctive peaks at binding energies of 284.80 eV, 286.47 eV, and 288.41 eV, assignable to C-C/C-H, C-O/C-N, and CO bands, respectively (Khare et al., 2018). The O1s high-resolution spectrum, shown in Fig. 1H, presents peaks at 531.52 eV and 533.29 eV, corresponding to CO and C-O bonds, respectively (Liang et al., 2022). Deconvolution of the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1I) reveals two characteristic peaks at 399.98 eV and 401.52 eV, attributed to C-N/pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively (Fu et al., 2022). Deconvolution of the Zr 3d spectrum in Fig. 1J resulted in two distinct peaks at 182.84 eV and 185.21 eV, which are attributed to Zr3d5/2 (Zr–O) and Zr3d3/2 (Zr-O) bonds (Su et al., 2020). The XPS findings are in good agreement with FTIR results, confirming the successful doping of CDs with heteroatoms and the synthesis of ZrN-CDs rich in functional groups. Surface carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups introduce a variety of emissive traps, which are crucial for the photoluminescence properties of the ZrN-CDs. Additionally, the presence of surface carboxyl groups is instrumental for coordinating with Eu3+ ions, providing a platform for subsequent applications. The zeta potential results displayed in Fig. 1K show that ZrN-CDs exhibit a negative potential, likely due to edge oxidation, while Eu3+ carries a positive potential, suggesting that they can bind through electrostatic interactions. The observed lower positive potential of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ complex compared to Eu3+ alone indicates successful conjugation of Eu3+ with ZrN-CDs, confirming the formation of a ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric system.

The optical characteristics of ZrN-CDs were further analyzed through UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2A illustrates a strong absorption peak at 352 nm in the absorption spectrum of ZrN-CDs, which is ascribed to the n-π* transition caused by CO bonds, resulting in strong fluorescent emission (Laddha et al., 2022). The inset presents a well-dispersed aqueous solution of ZrN-CDs, exhibiting intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The fluorescence spectra of ZrN-CDs were recorded across an excitation wavelength range from 300 nm to 370 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the emission peak positions of ZrN-CDs consistently appeared around 440 nm at various excitation wavelengths, with no significant shift and excitation dependence observed. The excitation-independent emission behavior of ZrN-CDs may be partly due to their uniform size and the uniform distribution of luminescent sites on their surfaces (Ai et al., 2021). Furthermore, an initial increase in fluorescence intensity was noted with increasing excitation wavelength, followed by a subsequent decrease. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 440 nm, thereby establishing the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths as 350 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence excitation-emission matrix in Fig. 2C revealing similar findings. The photobleaching resistance of ZrN-CDs was evaluated by monitoring their fluorescence intensity under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm) for 60 min. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged throughout the test, indicating that the ZrN-CDs exhibit excellent photobleaching resistance. At the optimal excitation wavelength, a quantum yield of 12.23 % was measured for ZrN-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanisms and feasibility of TET and OTC
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experimental studies were conducted to demonstrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism involving TCs and ZrN-CDs. Fig. 3A presents a comparison of the excitation and emission spectra of ZrN-CDs with the UV-Vis absorption spectra of TCs. Notably, there is a partial spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of TET and OTC and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of ZrN-CDs. This observation suggests that the quenching mechanism could be attributed to IFE or FRET. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer mechanism that depends on the distance between donor and acceptor molecules, typically occurring over distances less than 10 nm (Wang et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2023). Compared to the absorption spectra of TCs alone, the absorption spectra of the ZrN-CD and TCs mixture exhibit a red shift, indicating the formation of a complex between ZrN-CDs and TCs. The new absorption peaks still overlap with the emission spectrum of ZrN-CDs, suggesting that FRET may be responsible for the observed quenching (Huang et al., 2022). Additionally, unlike FRET, the IFE does not involve energy transfer between donor and acceptor molecules, typically resulting in unchanged fluorescence decay lifetimes (Chen et al., 2024). To further elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decay lifetimes of ZrN-CDs were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence lifetime of ZrN-CDs was originally 13.99 ns, which decreased to 4.16 ns and 7.84 ns upon the addition of TET and OTC at a concentration of 60 μM, respectively. This indicates that FRET presents as a quenching mechanism between TCs and ZrN-CDs.

Subsequent experiments assessed the feasibility of using a ZrN-CDs-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of tetracyclines (TCs), as depicted in Fig. 3C. The ZrN-CDs alone exhibited maximum fluorescence intensity at 440 nm (green curve). Following the addition of Eu3+, the fluorescence spectrum of the ZrN-CDs (purple curve) remained consistent with the emission spectrum of the ZrN-CDs, with no Eu3+ fluorescence observed. This phenomenon is attributed to quenching effects induced by the high-frequency vibrational coupling of water molecules coordinated with Eu3+. This result also suggests that the incorporation of Eu3+ ions have no discernible effect on the behavior of ZrN-CDs, allowing for the construction of a ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system. Upon the introduction of TET/OTC, the fluorescence emission of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system at 440 nm decreased, while a new characteristic emission emerged at 615 nm, exhibiting a dual-signal response pattern with inverse changes. The change in fluorescence emission at 440 nm is attributed to FRET between ZrN-CDs and TCs. The fluorescence enhancement at 615 nm is due to the β-diketone structure present in TCs, which can chelate with lanthanide ions (Eu3+) and act as an "antenna" to transfer energy to Eu3+ through AE, thereby inducing its luminescence (Ren et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2023). Consequently, this system allows for ratiometric determination of TCs based on opposite dual-emission signals.

To achieve optimal detection performance, parameters including ZrN-CDs concentration, Eu3+ concentration, the pH of the detection system, and reaction time were optimized, using TET as the target analyte.

The optimal ZrN-CDs concentration for the assay was determined by maximizing the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Q
E ), calculated as (Q
E =（F0-F）/F0), where (F0) is the fluorescence intensity of the system without the addition of TET, and (F) represents the fluorescence intensity after TET addition, both in arbitrary units (a.u.). Fig. 4A demonstrates that fluorescence Q
E increases sharply with rising ZrN-CDs concentrations until it stabilizes. The Q
E reaches its maxi mum at a ZrN-CDs concentration of 18 mg/L, thereby establishing 18 mg/L as the optimal concentration for the detection system.

The impact of Eu3+ concentration on the fluorescence response intensity was pronounced, necessitating the optimization of Eu3+ concentration within the system. Fig. 4B, reveals a gradual elevation in the F615/F440 ratio as the Eu3+ concentration rising from 20 μM to 60 μM, reaching a peak and subsequently decreasing for concentrations above 60 μM. Consequently, a concentration of 60 μM for Eu3+ was selected for establishing the ZrN-CDs/ Eu3+ detection system.

Subsequently, the pH of the reaction system was optimized. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, the ratio signal F615/F440 reaches its maximum in the pH range of 8.0–9.0. However, under highly alkaline and acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio decreases, likely due to the precipitation of europium hydroxide (Wei et al., 2019). In addition, we measured the zeta potential of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system under different pH conditions and found that it reached 31.2 mV at pH 8 (Fig. S1). A zeta potential greater than 30 mV ensures sufficient electrostatic repulsion within the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system, maintaining its colloidal stability (Fiuza et al., 2019). This stability may be one of the reasons why the reaction system achieves optimal detection performance at pH 8. After comprehensive consideration, a pH of 8.0 was chosen for the detection system.

Finally, to achieve optimal detection efficiency, the reaction time between the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system and the target was optimized. Fig. 4D illustrates that the F615/F440 ratio increases rapidly upon reaction initiation, reaching equilibrium within 3 min and maintaining stability. This indicates a rapid coordination reaction between ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ and TET, as well as the high stability of the formed complex. Therefore, for subsequent TCs fluorescence determination, a ZrN-CDs concentration of 18 mg/L, an Eu3+ concentration of 60 μM, a system pH of 8, and a reaction time of 3 min were selected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorometric detection for TET and OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized detection conditions, standard solutions of the most common TCs in milk (TET and OTC) were sequentially added to the assay system to establish a linear relationship between TCs concentration and the F615/F440 ratio signal. As depicted in Fig. 5A and C, a significant fluorescence emission from ZrN-CDs (F440) was observed before the addition of TCs with no significant emission from Eu3+ (F615). With increasing TCs concentrations, F440 continuously decreased while F615 increased. This opposite dual-emission response signals that the fluorescent probe could be exploited for a ratiometric determination method for TCs. The ratio signal F615/F440 reached saturation at a TCs concentration of 70 μM, beyond which an increase in TCs concentration resulted in a decrease rather than an increase in F615 due to saturation in the formation of Eu3+-TCs complexes (Yang et al., 2020). As demonstrated in Fig. 5B, TET exhibited a robust linear relationship with F615/F440 over the concentration ranges of 0.25–20 μM and 20–70 μM, with the linear equations being F615/F440 = 0.0419CTET+0.0125 (R2 = 0.996) and F615/F440 = 0.0252CTET+0.2998 (R2=0.999) respectively, and the calculated detected limit of detection (LOD) was 0.029 μM (LOD = 3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of 10 replicate measurements of the blank sample, k is the slope of the linear equation). For OTC, as depicted in Fig. 5D, F615/F440 linearly increased with OTC concentrations in the ranges of 0.25–20 μM and 20–70 μM. The corresponding linear equations were F615/F440 = 0.0506COTC+0.0164 (R2 = 0.979) and F615/F440 = 0.0272COTC+0.5592 (R2 = 0.993), with the calculated LOD being 0.016 μM. Compared to previously reported sensing methods for TCs (as shown in Table 1), this approach exhibits a lower LOD and a broader linear detection range, with LODs below the EU specified maximum residue limits for TET and OTC in milk (0.225 μM for TET and 0.217 μM for OTC). Moreover, this fluorescent sensor offers a rapid, simple, and low-cost assay procedure without the need for complex preparation or special reaction media. The detection procedure involves only ZrN-CDs synthesized using citric acid, zirconium chloride, and ethylenediamine as precursors, along with Eu3+ solution, Tris-HCl buffer, and signal measurement via a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Considering these costs, the approximate cost of a single test is estimated to be less than $0.001. With commercialization and large-scale production, the detection cost of the fluorescent sensor could be further reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and anti-interference of the ratio fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In complex milk samples, Eu3+ may coordinate with other molecules, potentially interfering with detection results. To further validate the specific response performance of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ system, other antibiotics (KAN, GEN, CHL, FLO, SMD, AMP, NEO) and typical interfering substances in milk (Ser, Arg, Tyr, Cys, Trp, AA, Glu, Gal, Lac) were individually introduced into the detection system at concentrations 5-fold higher than TET and OTC. Fig. 6 illustrates that the potential other antibiotics and high-concentration interferences caused only slight changes in the F615/F440 signal. Notably, significant ratio signal changes were only observed upon the introduction of TET/OTC, confirming the high specificity of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ detection system for TET/OTC. To exclude the impact of other types of metal ions on this detection system, the ratio fluorescence response to metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+) was evaluated. The results demonstrated that even at 5-fold higher concentrations of these metal ions, the fluorescence detection of TET/OTC remained unaffected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TET residue in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the feasibility of the sensor for practical application in milk samples, bovine, goat, and camel milk samples were pre-treated and then spiked with varying concentrations of TET (5, 10, and 20 µM) followed by detection using the developed ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescence sensor method. Table 2 reveals that the recovery rates of TET in milk varied from 90.91 % to 113.19 %, with all relative standard deviations (RSD) being less than 3.75 %. Additionally, the detection results of the sensor were in close agreement with those from the standard method HPLC, demonstrating that the proposed sensor has reliability and precision for TET detection in milk. This substantiates its utility for analyzing TCs in actual milk samples.

In this study, ZrN-CDs with excellent fluorescence performance and low cost were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The occurrence of FRET effects between ZrN-CDs and TCs was confirmed. The study confirmed the existence of a FRET effect between ZrN-CDs and TCs, resulting in significant quenching of ZrN-CDs fluorescence emission at 440 nm. Additionally, AE enhanced the fluorescence response of Eu3+ at 615 nm in the presence of TCs. Considering this effect, a ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric detection system was developed by mixing Eu3+ with ZrN-CDs, which was then applied for the highly sensitive and selective determination of TET and OTC. With increasing concentrations of TCs, the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ sensor demonstrated opposite fluorescence signal changes at 440 nm and 615 nm, respectively. Quantification of TET and OTC was achieved by recording the F615/F440 fluorescence intensity ratio, establishing standard curves with linear ranges of 0.25–70 μM. The limits of detection (LODs) were calculated to be 0.029 µM for TET and 0.016 µM for OTC, both below the maximum residue limits for TET (0.225 μM) and OTC (0.217 μM) in milk set by the European Union. Recovery tests for TET in spiked milk, goat milk, and camel milk samples demonstrated recoveries ranging from 90.91 % to 113.19 %, confirming the applicability of the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ probe for TC detection in real samples. Moreover, the fluorescence response measurement of TCs by the ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ probe can be completed within 3 min, with a calculated cost per measurement of approximately $0.0048. This work presents an approach with potential for rapid, on-site TCs detection, offering low cost and portability. Despite these promising properties, the synthesis of ZrN-CDs/Eu3+ has predominantly been confined to laboratory settings, and scaling up production remains challenging. Continued efforts to address these issues could potentially lead to more practical and widespread sensing applications, given the excellent optical properties of ZrN-CDs/Eu3+. In terms of application, significant efforts are required in the miniaturization and microfabrication of fluorescent sensors to meet the demands of measurements outside the laboratory environment.
